text: Drape should first be placed along the spinous processes posteriorly and in the midline anteriorly and then just under the clavicle on superior scapular border on top taking care to exclude the axillary region
and then above the iliac crest on the bottom.
It is useful to mark the line further than anticipated if there is a need during the procedure to extend the incision.
Here we are cutting through the skin.
Gauze is used to absorb any bleeding to keep the field clear.
Then using the diathermy, the subcutaneous layer is divided down to the muscle.
Care is taken to ensure the layer is divided to the very end of the incision.
Again, hemostasis is achieved using the diathermy to cauterize any points of bleeding.

The first muscle we encounter in a posterolateral thoracotomy is the latissimus dorsi, which is located laterally and extends from the inferior border of the scapula to the spinous processes of the vertebrae.
It becomes the thoracolumbar fascia at the auscultatory triangle.
the litissimus dorsi is completely divided.
For the serratus sparing technique, it is divided from posterior to anterior.
When dividing the anterior part, a hand is placed under the litissimus to protect the underlying serratus anterior muscle.
Here we can see one of the slips of the serratus muscle.
In this video we demonstrate the serratus sparing technique.
In order to preserve the serratus muscle, the fascia around the serratus anterior is dissected.
The fascia is lifted up using a pair of Bonnie forceps with the assistant using DeBakey forceps to keep the muscle out of the way.
but not into the muscle itself to allow for maximal retraction of the serratus anterior to improve exposure.
If you do not wish to spare the serratus muscle, then the muscle can be completely divided in the line of the incision.
Here, we can also see some fibers of the trapezius and the rhomboid located posteriorly.
These are also divided.
Before entering the chest, the scapula is retracted and the rib spaces are palpated and carefully counted to identify the fifth intercostal space.
Note that the first space that can be felt is usually the second intercostal space due to the attachment of the scalene muscles on the second rib.
The diathermy is used to divide the tissue overlying the superior border of the sixth rib and mark the periosteum before dividing the intercostals.
This helps to guide the intercostal division in the right plane and enable a smooth incision through all the intercostal muscles and pleura.
This is done anteriorly as far forward as possible and posteriorly up to the erector spinae muscles.
which can be seen here nicely as a white tendinous band running vertically.
Breach the pleural cavity initially in a small section to allow the lung to fall away due to the creation of a pneumothorax, which can be seen here.

Then you can insert your finger into the incision to push back and protect the lung whilst extending the cut from back to front, again up to the erector spinae muscles posteriorly and then as anteriorly as possible.
Here you can clearly see the incision and the collapsed lung.
The incision is extended back to front to go along the acute angle of the intercostal fibres, which allows for a clean cut along the margin of the rib.
To aim spreading of the rib in a controlled way, divide a segment of the sixth rib.
Dissect on the lower border of the sixth rib using the diathermy to char the segment of bone that is to be divided.
Insert a Roberts just under the sixth rib, carefully angled upwards to avoid injury to the underlying lung, and spread the area which has been dissected to create a space about 1-2 cm in length.
Then use the diathermy to burn the borders of the segment to be divided on the bone.
Then insert the rib cutters into the space and cut away a small 2 cm segment of bone.
This prevents an uncontrolled fracture elsewhere at the rib.
Rib spreaders are then placed ensuring the blades are under all the rib tissue and slowly opened up to allow for gentle and controlled spreading of the ribs.
This gives tissues time to expand and minimizes injury.
Here's a nice view of the heart with the spreader in place.
A Langenbach retractor is used to pull the serratus muscle forward and the incision is then internally extended anteriorly with the diathermy.
The incision can be extended up to the internal mammary vessels.
You can see the internally extended incision here.
This allows for more spreading and at this point the spreader can be further expanded.
A stay stitch is then placed into the serratus muscle to retract it forward as anteriorly as possible and then tied onto the retractors.
This optimizes the exposure whilst minimizing the incision and trauma to the tissues.
